elements.sent-2411-001: As software systems grow in complexity, data and tools that provide valuable insights for easier program comprehension become increasingly important.
elements.sent-2411-002: OpenTelemetry has become a standard for the collection of monitoring data.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work we present our experiences with different ways how OpenTelemetry can be leveraged to automatically instrument software systems for the purpose of software visualization.
elements.sent-2411-004: Particularly, we explore automatic instrumentation with the OpenTelemetry SDKs, and both application and unit test instrumentation with the Java agent inspectIT Ocelot .
elements.sent-2411-005: The collected data is exported to our live trace visualization tool ExplorViz.
elements.sent-2411-006: Monitoring data can be used to gain valuable insight into the behavior of software systems and thereby facilitate program comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-007: OpenTelemetry is a collection of APIs, SDKs, and tools for software observability, and the de facto standard for enabling interoperability between tools that collect or process monitoring data~\cite{janes2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper we report on different ways how OpenTelemetry can be leveraged to collect tracing data.
elements.sent-2411-009: In particular, we take a look at OpenTelemetry's own automatic instrumentation and both application and unit test monitoring with the inspectIT Ocelot\footnote{\url{https://www.inspectit.rocks/ Java agent.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The instrumentation process and the resulting data are put into perspective from the point of view of our software visualization tool ExplorViz.
elements.sent-2411-011: The rest of this paper is organized as follows: In the next section we describe our software visualization approach.
elements.sent-2411-012: We then look at three approaches for the collection of OpenTelemetry-compliant monitoring data in the context of software visualization.
elements.sent-2411-013: Afterwards, we present previous publications which are related to our presented approaches.
elements.sent-2411-014: We conclude the paper with a summary and brief outlook.
elements.sent-2411-015: ExplorViz~\cite{hasselbring2020explorviz is our web-based software visualization tool which employs the city metaphor~\cite{wettel2007 .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Up until a few years ago, Kieker~\cite{hasselbring2020kieker was used as the sole monitoring tool for ExplorViz.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The compatibility between the two tools was lost when ExplorViz switched to the emerging and popular OpenTelemetry~\cite{Blanco2023 standard for tracing as part of a major restructuring of ExplorViz.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In Figure \ref{fig:workflow the conceptual architecture of ExplorViz and the flow of monitoring data is depicted.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Running applications are instrumented with an OpenTelemetry compliant agent in the monitoring stage.
elements.sent-2411-020: The data is then exported via gRPC to an OpenTelemetry Collector which exports it to the backend of ExplorViz via Apache Kafka.\footnote{\url{https://kafka.apache.org/ The retrieved tracing data is processed in the analysis stage, where traces and structural data of the software system is reconstructed from the retrieved OpenTelemetry spans.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: At last, both the processed dynamic and static data is requested and visualized by the frontend.
elements.sent-2411-022: The resulting visualization by the example of a distributed version of the Spring PetClinic\footnote{\url{https://github.com/spring-petclinic/spring-petclinic-microservices is presented in Figure \ref{fig:explorviz .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Applications are depicted by gray foundations.
elements.sent-2411-024: The hierarchical package structure is depicted by green and blue components which can be interpreted as districts of the software city.
elements.sent-2411-025: Components can contain classes, which are represented by blue rectangular cuboids and can be interpreted as buildings.
elements.sent-2411-026: The communication, i.e. method calls, which occured in the observed time frame are depicted with yellow arcs.
elements.sent-2411-027: The visualization can be explored collaboratively and supports diverse devices including augmented and virtual reality headsets via WebXR.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/ExplorViz-Data-Flow.pdf} \caption{Simplified architecture diagram of ExplorViz depicting data flows.} \label{fig:workflow}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this section we present different ways to monitor software systems for the purpose of software visualization.
elements.sent-2411-029: First, we take a look at the automatic instrumentation solution provided by OpenTelemetry before we present application and unit test monitoring with the third party Java agent inspectIT Ocelot.
elements.sent-2411-030: Aside from the option to manually instrument an application, OpenTelemetry provides eleven language SDKs to automatically collect traces of software systems, whereby the SDK for Rust is in a beta status at the time of writing.
elements.sent-2411-031: It is noteworthy that the automatic instrumentation of those SDKs is used solely for distributed tracing.
elements.sent-2411-032: Therefore, function calls of software libraries and frameworks that handle distributed events such as HTTP requests or I/O-operations may be caught.
elements.sent-2411-033: For ExplorViz, we explored the reference implementation for the instrumentation of JavaScript applications by using mongo-express\footnote{\url{https://github.com/mongo-express/mongo-express as a Node.js\footnote{\url{https://nodejs.org , and the Angular frontend for the Spring PetClinic~\footnote{\url{https://github.com/spring-petclinic/spring-petclinic-angular as a web-based JavaScript application as examples.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: As implied by the distributed focus of the SDKs, function calls that occur solely within an application, often including most parts of the business logic, are usually not captured.
elements.sent-2411-035: As there is also no information about the source code file that the function call originates from, cluster algorithms would be needed to create an artificial software structure.
elements.sent-2411-036: On its own, the solely distributed instrumentation is not sufficient for a comprehensive visualization with ExplorViz, since our visualization approach is focused around the internal behavior and structure by means of the software city metaphor.
elements.sent-2411-037: On the other hand, internal function calls can be monitored with Kieker or inspectIT Ocelot, which is considered in the two upcoming sections.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth/2]{figures/distributed-pet-clinic.png} \caption{Distributed version of the Spring PetClinic as visualized by ExplorViz with data from the inspectIT Ocelot agent.} \label{fig:explorviz}
elements.sent-2411-038: As ExplorViz is built upon the city metaphor, collecting monitoring data about the inner structure and behavior of a software system is the key to a comprehensive visualization.
elements.sent-2411-039: The Java agent inspectIT Ocelot can dynamically instrument Java applications and export the collected monitoring data in accordance with the OpenTelemetry standard.
elements.sent-2411-040: The agent can be started with a given software or added to a running process of the java virtual machine.
elements.sent-2411-041: For easy deployment, all components of ExplorViz are developed with continuous integration and continuous delivery as Docker containers in mind.
elements.sent-2411-042: Therefore, to instrument a given software, the inspectIT Ocelot agent along with a configuration for instrumentation is mounted into the software's container.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the end, only the container's entrypoint needs to be adapted to start with the agent, leaving the monitored software untouched and resulting in an automatic instrumentation process.
elements.sent-2411-044: An example of a software landscape visualized using this monitoring approach is presented in Figure \ref{fig:explorviz .}
elements.sent-2411-045: As can be seen, this approach has the advantage that both internal and distributed communication is captured.
elements.sent-2411-046: Therefore, this is the current default monitoring approach for ExplorViz.
elements.sent-2411-047: As a third option for monitoring software systems, we propose the instrumentation of unit tests.
elements.sent-2411-048: The setup of the previous section can be adapted such that inspectIT Ocelot is started with the test suite, for example, as part of a Gradle project.
elements.sent-2411-049: This could be used as a supplement to static code analysis as part of continuous integration to detect unexpected changes in program behavior beyond the binary results of unit tests.
elements.sent-2411-050: However, this approach comes with some caveats.
elements.sent-2411-051: From a technical perspective, the Java agent needs to be fully loaded before tests are executed to avoid that test cases are missed.
elements.sent-2411-052: The execution of all unit tests can then result in millions of spans in a short amount of time for large projects like Spring for Apache Kafka.\footnote{\url{https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-kafka Therefore, it can easily happen that spans are dropped, for example at the OpenTelemetry Collector, which acts as a buffer.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: More fundamentally, this approach relies on the expressiveness and comparability of unit tests, which can vary greatly from project to project~\cite{reichelt2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Kieker~\cite{Kieker2012,hasselbring2020kieker is a tool that provides dynamic analysis of software systems.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Just as inspectIT Ocelot, it provides data about the function calls that occur both inside and between applications.
elements.sent-2411-056: As opposed to only being able to instrument Java applications, Kieker supports several programming languages such as C++ and has included components to analyze the gathered monitoring data.
elements.sent-2411-057: On the other hand, as of now, Kieker uses its own data format for monitoring data and thus is not compatible with tools that expect OpenTelemetry spans, such as ExplorViz.
elements.sent-2411-058: However, it is an ongoing effort to restore the compatibility between Kieker and ExplorViz by adding an OpenTelemetry compliant exporter to Kieker.
elements.sent-2411-059: Reichelt et al.~\cite{reichelt2021overhead compared the overhead of OpenTelemetry, inspectIT, and Kieker.}
elements.sent-2411-060: They observed that Kieker is creating slightly less overhead than inspectIT and OpenTelemetry when processing traces.
elements.sent-2411-061: Reichelt~\cite{reichelt2023 also examined performance changes on source code level.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Just as we did, he also instrumented unit tests to gain insights into the software system.
elements.sent-2411-063: However, he used Kieker as opposed to inspectIT Ocelot and had a greater focus on regression testing for performance than the behavior-driven software visualization approach that we pursue.
elements.sent-2411-064: We presented three approaches to gather tracing data from software systems in accordance with the OpenTelemetry standard.
elements.sent-2411-065: We looked at the resulting data from the point of view of our software visualization tool ExplorViz, which is centered around the metaphor of software cities.
elements.sent-2411-066: Both the simple to setup automatic instrumentation of OpenTelemetry and the instrumentation of unit tests have distinct use cases.
elements.sent-2411-067: However, our experience has shown that a monitoring tool which can automatically track both distributed and internal traces is best suited for our visualization approach.
elements.sent-2411-068: As of now, the Java agent inspectIT Ocelot is the best fit for ExplorViz.
elements.sent-2411-069: We plan to extend the support for OpenTelemetry of ExplorViz to also include metrics and deployment information such as data about Kubernetes clusters.
elements.sent-2411-070: At last, we are looking forward to regain full interoperability between ExplorViz and Kieker, to leverage its powerful monitoring capabilities and eventually use it in place of inspectIT Ocelot.
elements.sent-2411-071: We want to thank Roman Hemens and Piet Schumacher for their contributions to ExplorViz regarding OpenTelemetry.
